message.content:
{
  "homework": {
    "sessionInformation": {
      "sessionNumber": "1",
      "sessionTitle": "Cell and Cell Organelles - Session 1",
      "subject": "Science",
      "grade": "Class IX",
      "difficultyLevel": "Balanced",
      "learningPace": "Balanced",
      "estimatedHomeworkDuration": "45 minutes"
    },
    "homework": [
      {
        "id": 1,
        "type": "Diagram Labelling and Identification",
        "title": "The Basic Unit of Life: Animal Cell Structure",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Identify the main components of a cell"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Cell Theory basics", "Animal Cell structure"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 5,
        "estimatedTime": "10 minutes",
        "instructions": "Based on today's lesson about animal cells, draw a neat labelled diagram of an Animal Cell. Ensure you include the following parts: Cell Membrane, Cytoplasm, Nucleus (with Nuclear Envelope and Nucleolus), Mitochondria, Ribosomes, Endoplasmic Reticulum (rough and smooth if covered), Golgi Apparatus, Lysosome, and Centrosome.",
        "question": "Draw a labelled diagram of an Animal Cell. Label at least 8 distinct organelles discussed in class today.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Student to draw on paper",
        "visualRequirement": "A clear, hand-drawn circular/oval shape representing the cell membrane with internal structures clearly distinguished and labelled using straight lines. No shading required unless specified by teacher.",
        "expectedResponse": "Accurate representation of an animal cell without a cell wall or chloroplasts. All labels must be correct."
      },
      {
        "id": 2,
        "type": "Matching",
        "title": "Organelle Functions Match-Up",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Understand the function of key organelles"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Cell Organelles and their functions"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 4,
        "estimatedTime": "8 minutes",
        "instructions": "Match each cell organelle listed in Column A with its correct primary function described in Column B. Write the corresponding letter next to the number.",
        "question": "Column A: (1) Mitochondria, (2) Nucleus, (3) Ribosomes, (4) Cell Membrane\nColumn B: (A) Controls all cellular activities and contains DNA; (B) Site of protein synthesis; (C) Regulates entry and exit of substances; (D) Powerhouse of the cell.",
        "options": [
          {"id": 1, "text": "(1)-D, (2)-A, (3)-B, (4)-C"},
          {"id": 2, "text": "(1)-A, (2)-D, (3)-B, (4)-C"},
          {"id": 3, "text": "(1)-D, (2)-B, (3)-A, (4)-C"}
        ],
        "answerSpace": "Write the correct letter sequence",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "Option A: (1)-D, (2)-A, (3)-B, (4)-C"
      },
      {
        "id": 3,
        "type": "Short Answer / Conceptual Reasoning",
        "title": "The Control Center of the Cell",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Explain why the nucleus is called the control center"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Nucleus function and importance"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 3,
        "estimatedTime": "7 minutes",
        "instructions": "In your own words, explain why scientists refer to the nucleus as the 'control center' or 'brain' of the cell. Mention what specific component inside it is responsible for this role.",
        "question": "Why is the Nucleus called the control center of the cell? What does it contain that makes this possible?",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "2-3 sentences",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "The nucleus controls all cellular activities because it contains DNA (genetic material). This genetic information directs protein synthesis and cell division, effectively managing the cell's functions."
      },
      {
        "id": 4,
        "type": "Real-life Application / Comparison",
        "title": "Cell Membrane: The Gatekeeper",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Understand selective permeability"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Cell Membrane properties"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 3,
        "estimatedTime": "7 minutes",
        "instructions": "The cell membrane is described as 'selectively permeable'. Explain what this means in the context of a living organism. Give one example of how this property helps an animal cell survive.",
        "question": "Explain the term 'Selectively Permeable' regarding the Cell Membrane and provide one real-life biological reason why this is essential for survival.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Short paragraph",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "'Selectively permeable' means only certain substances can pass through while others are blocked. This is essential because it allows nutrients to enter and waste products to exit, maintaining the internal balance (homeostasis) of the cell."
      },
      {
        "id": 5,
        "type": "True/False with Correction",
        "title": "Fact Check: Cell Basics",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Recall basic facts about cells"],
        "topicCoverage": ["General Cell Theory and Structure"],
        "difficulty": "Easy to Balanced",
        "marks": 3,
        "estimatedTime": "6 minutes",
        "instructions": "Read the following statements. Mark them as True or False. If a statement is False, rewrite it correctly.",
        "question": "(a) All living organisms are made of cells.\n(b) The cell wall provides flexibility to animal cells.\n(c) Mitochondria produce energy for the cell.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "True/False and correction if needed",
        "visualRequirement": "",
        "expectedResponse": "(a) True. (b) False - The cell wall is absent in animal cells; it provides rigidity to plant cells. (c) True."
      },
      {
        "id": 6,
        "type": "Home Experiment / Observation",
        "title": "Observing Cells at Home: Onion Peel or Cheek Swab",
        "learningOutcomeIds": ["Observe cell structure practically"],
        "topicCoverage": ["Practical observation of cells"],
        "difficulty": "Balanced",
        "marks": 2,
        "estimatedTime": "10 minutes (plus preparation time)",
        "instructions": "If you have a simple microscope or can use a smartphone macro lens: Prepare a temporary mount of an onion peel OR gently scrape the inside of your cheek. Observe under low power and sketch what you see in 2-3 lines, noting if you can distinguish any boundaries (like cell walls for onion). If no equipment is available, write down how you would perform this experiment safely at home.",
        "question": "Perform a safe observation activity to look for cells. Sketch the shape of the cells observed or describe the steps you would take using household items and basic safety precautions.",
        "options": [],
        "answerSpace": "Sketch or written procedure",
        "visualRequirement": "A simple sketch showing rectangular shapes (onion) or irregular shapes (cheek), OR a bulleted list of materials needed for home observation.",
        "expectedResponse": "Student provides either a rough sketch of observed cells or a safe, logical plan to observe cheek/onion cells using available resources."
      }
    ],
    "summary": {
      "totalQuestions": 6,
      "totalMarks": 20,
      "estimatedCompletionTime": "48 minutes",
      "learningOutcomesCovered": [
        "Identify the main components of a cell",
        "Understand the function of key organelles",
        "Explain why the nucleus is called the control center",
        "Understand selective permeability"
      ],
      "topicsCovered": [
        "Cell Theory basics",
        "Animal Cell structure",
        "Nucleus function and importance",
        "Cell Membrane properties",
        "General Cell Theory and Structure",
        "Practical observation of cells"
      ],
      "subtopicsCovered": [
        "Mitochondria, Nucleus, Ribosomes, Cell Membrane functions",
        "Selective permeability concept",
        "Cell wall vs. Cell membrane distinction (Animal)",
        "Home-based cell observation techniques"
      ],
      "taskDistribution": {
        "Diagram Labelling and Identification": 1,
        "Matching": 1,
        "Short Answer / Conceptual Reasoning": 2,
        "Real-life Application / Comparison": 1,
        "True/False with Correction": 1,
        "Home Experiment / Observation": 1
      },
      "homeExperimentIncluded": true,
      "parentEngagementIncluded": false
    }
  }
}
